1.zoneStates: Far North QLD, NT Top End, Kimberley WA
1.summary: This climate has no real winter. The challenge is year-round heat and high humidity, with a distinct wet and dry season. Cooling, shade, and ventilation do all the work — thermal mass is your enemy here, not your friend.

principles.title: Cross-ventilation is your primary cooling tool
principles.body: Open your house to the prevailing breeze. In most of Zone 1, the dominant breeze is from the SE in the dry season and NW in the wet. Place openable windows on opposite sides of each room — not just opposite walls of the house. The airflow through a room matters more than the total window area. Louvres and high-level clerestory windows that vent hot air from ceiling level are highly effective. Ceiling fans in every room reduce the felt temperature by 3–4°C at minimal energy cost.

principles.title: Raise the floor off the ground
principles.body: Traditional Queensland-style homes on stumps or posts catch breezes at floor level and prevent ground heat and moisture from entering the building. Even a 600mm raise makes a measurable difference to ventilation and comfort. Avoid slab-on-ground in this zone — concrete slabs absorb and radiate heat around the clock.

principles.title: Shade the walls and roof before anything else
principles.body: The single most effective intervention in Zone 1 is preventing solar radiation from hitting your walls and roof in the first place. Deep verandahs on all sides (minimum 2m wide), overhanging eaves, and shade trees planted on the east and west flanks do more than any amount of insulation. The roof is your biggest heat gain surface — a well-ventilated roof cavity with reflective foil under the roof cladding dramatically reduces ceiling heat load.

principles.title: Avoid heavy thermal mass
principles.body: Brick, concrete, and stone store heat during the day and release it at night. In Zone 1, nights are rarely cool enough to flush this stored heat away — so thermal mass just makes your house hotter around the clock. Lightweight construction (timber frame, fibre cement, or steel) that does not store heat is preferred. If you are building in masonry for other reasons, ensure the walls are well-insulated on the outside face.

principles.title: Minimise east and west glazing
principles.body: East and west walls receive low-angle morning and afternoon sun that is almost impossible to shade with fixed eaves — the sun hits too close to horizontal. Keep windows on east and west walls small and use external shading devices such as vertical fins or deciduous pergolas. If you have a choice, put living areas and bedrooms on the south or north sides of the house and treat east/west facades as utility zones.

principles.title: Choose a light-coloured roof
principles.body: A light or reflective roof surface can reduce the roof's radiant heat load by up to 40% compared to a dark metal roof. In Zone 1, this is one of the cheapest and most impactful decisions you can make at the time of construction or re-roofing.

2.zoneStates: Coastal QLD (Cairns to Brisbane), coastal NT, coastal Pilbara WA
2.summary: Similar to Zone 1 but with slightly more seasonal variation — winters are mild rather than hot. Cooling still dominates the challenge, but there are occasional cool nights where thermal mass can provide some benefit. Ventilation and shading remain the primary strategies.

principles.title: Design for airflow as the first priority
principles.body: Zone 2 homes need to breathe. Orient the house to capture the dominant local breeze (typically SE trade winds along the Queensland coast). Rooms should be no more than two deep from an external wall with openable windows. Avoid internal corridors that block airflow. Use louvred windows or operable panels that can stay open safely during wet weather.

principles.title: Face living areas north — but shade aggressively
principles.body: Unlike Zone 1, Zone 2 has a mild winter where some passive solar gain in the morning is welcome. North-facing living areas with generous eaves (900mm–1200mm) allow low winter sun in while blocking high summer sun. The eave sizing matters: too short and you get summer overheating, too long and you lose winter light. Use the latitude-based rule: eave projection = distance from eave to window sill × tan(90° − latitude − 23.5°) for summer cut-off.

principles.title: Treat east and west facades as utility walls
principles.body: Western sun in the afternoon causes the most uncomfortable heat gain in Zone 2. Minimise window openings on the west wall, or protect them with deep vertical fins, pergolas, or deciduous climbing plants. East windows are less problematic in the morning but still warrant shading in summer. Plant shade trees on the west side — a single large tree can shade an entire western wall by afternoon.

principles.title: Use limited thermal mass in living areas only
principles.body: A modest amount of thermal mass in north-facing living areas (exposed concrete slab, tiled floor over slab, or a masonry feature wall) can moderate daily temperature swings. The mass absorbs warmth on cool winter days and stays cool on summer mornings before heating up. The critical condition: it must be shaded from direct sun in summer or it becomes a heat battery. Keep bedrooms lightweight so they cool down quickly at night.

principles.title: Insulate the roof above all else
principles.body: The roof receives solar radiation on its entire surface throughout the day. R4.1 minimum insulation in the ceiling (R5+ preferred) combined with a reflective sarking layer under the roof cladding dramatically reduces heat entering the living space. A well-ventilated roof cavity between the insulation and the roof cladding exhausts trapped heat. This is often the single highest-return investment in a Zone 2 home.

principles.title: Specify low-SHGC glazing for east and west windows
principles.body: If you cannot avoid east or west glazing, specify glass with a Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC) below 0.4. This limits the amount of solar energy that passes through while still allowing daylight. Low-e coatings on glass also reduce radiant heat transfer. For north-facing windows where you want some winter sun gain, a higher SHGC (0.5–0.6) combined with good eave shading is appropriate.

3.zoneStates: Inland QLD, inland NSW, northern SA, inland WA (Gascoyne, Murchison)
3.summary: Extreme summer heat with low humidity, and mild to warm winters. The significant daily temperature swing (often 15–20°C between day and night) is a major asset — thermal mass charged with cool night air stays comfortable well into the next day. Both heating and cooling matter here.

principles.title: Use thermal mass with night purge ventilation
principles.body: Zone 3's biggest advantage is the large diurnal temperature range — nights are often 15–20°C cooler than afternoons. A concrete slab or masonry walls absorb heat during the day but can be flushed with cool night air by opening windows in the evening and closing them in the morning. This passive cooling cycle can keep indoor temperatures 8–12°C below outdoor peak temperatures. Concrete floors, brick walls, and rammed earth all perform well here.

principles.title: North-facing glazing with correctly sized eaves
principles.body: Living areas should face north. In winter, north-facing glass allows the low sun to enter and warm the thermal mass inside. In summer, correctly proportioned eaves exclude the high sun entirely. The eave calculation is critical here because summer temperatures are extreme — undersized eaves cause severe overheating. A general rule for inland Australia: eave overhang should equal approximately 45% of the window height for full summer exclusion at noon.

principles.title: Exclude summer sun completely — then open at night
principles.body: The primary summer strategy is to treat the house as a sealed cool box during the day. Close all windows, blinds, and doors before temperatures rise in the morning. Shade all glazing with eaves, external blinds, or vegetation. Then open everything up at night to flush heat out and charge the thermal mass with cool air. This two-phase daily cycle dramatically reduces or eliminates the need for air conditioning.

principles.title: Add external shading to all east and west windows
principles.body: East and west glazing is the hardest to shade with fixed eaves because the morning and afternoon sun is at a low angle. Use external roller blinds, fixed vertical fins, or deciduous pergolas for these orientations. Internal blinds are largely ineffective — by the time the heat reaches an internal blind, it is already inside the glass and entering the room. External shading that stops the sun before it hits the glass is the only effective approach.

principles.title: Evaporative cooling works well in this climate
principles.body: Zone 3's low humidity makes evaporative coolers far more effective than in humid zones. An evaporative cooler uses roughly one-eighth the electricity of a refrigerative air conditioner for similar cooling output. It also requires open windows or vents to push air through the house — which complements passive design principles. Evaporative cooling is not effective on the rare humid days but handles the vast majority of hot days in this zone very well.

principles.title: Design for passive solar heating in winter
principles.body: Winters in Zone 3 are mild but can include cold nights and mornings. North-facing glass that admits winter sun onto thermal mass is effective free heating. Ensure there are no obstructions (fences, trees, neighbouring buildings) blocking winter sun from the north face. Deciduous trees on the north side can provide summer shade while losing their leaves to allow winter sun through — an elegant dual-season solution.

4.zoneStates: Adelaide, inland Victoria, ACT, southern inland NSW, Perth Hills
4.summary: Defined by hot dry summers and cold winters. Both heating and cooling loads are significant and roughly balanced. Passive solar design directly addresses both seasons, making orientation and thermal mass particularly important. This is the climate where good passive design gives the highest reward.

principles.title: North orientation is non-negotiable here
principles.body: Zone 4 is where passive solar design was essentially invented as a concept. A north-facing home with correctly designed eaves provides free heating in winter and automatic shade in summer without any mechanical systems. The sun arc in this zone is dramatically different between summer (high, stays north) and winter (low, only spans the northern sky) — making fixed eave overhangs a precise engineering solution. Living areas, kitchen, and bedrooms should all be on the north side. Garages, laundries, and bathrooms belong on the south.

principles.title: Size your eaves precisely for your latitude
principles.body: The eave overhang for north-facing windows should allow winter sun to strike the floor at least 3m inside the room for effective thermal mass charging, while blocking the summer sun completely at solar noon. For Adelaide (lat 35°S): an eave of 600–700mm over a 2.1m high window is the target. For Canberra (lat 35°S): similar. For the Perth Hills (lat 32°S): slightly shorter eaves work. If you provide your suburb, the sun path arc on this tool shows you exactly where the sun hits at summer and winter solstice to verify your eave design.

principles.title: Maximise thermal mass in north-facing rooms
principles.body: Thermal mass in north-facing rooms is essential in Zone 4. The mass absorbs solar energy through winter days and releases it through winter nights — dramatically reducing heating costs. Concrete slabs exposed to winter sun, polished concrete floors, brick feature walls on the north face, and rammed earth walls all perform well. The rule of thumb: thermal mass should have a surface area of at least 6 times the area of the north-facing glazing it is receiving sun from. Mass located more than 4m from the glazing is much less effective.

principles.title: High insulation is essential for both seasons
principles.body: Zone 4 requires high insulation levels in the roof, walls, and floor because both summer heat and winter cold need to be kept out. Minimum ceiling insulation: R5.0–R6.0. Wall insulation: R2.5–R3.0. Concrete slab edge insulation prevents the thermal mass from leaking stored heat to the outdoors. Double glazing is worth serious consideration in Zone 4 — single glazing loses enormous amounts of heat in winter and allows significant solar gain in summer even with external shading.

principles.title: Minimise south-facing glazing
principles.body: South-facing windows receive almost no direct sun in Zone 4 yet allow enormous heat loss in winter. Every square metre of single-glazed south window loses roughly 4–5 times more heat than an insulated wall in the same position. Limit south-facing windows to small openings for ventilation and emergency egress only. If you have views or prevailing breezes from the south, consider double or triple glazing and make the openings no larger than necessary.

principles.title: Use night purge ventilation through summer
principles.body: Zone 4 summers are hot but nights are reliably cool. Open all windows in the evening to flush the day's accumulated heat, then close the house in the morning before the temperature climbs. This cycle works best when the thermal mass is significant enough to absorb the incoming heat across a full day. A well-designed Zone 4 home can maintain indoor temperatures of 24–26°C on 40°C days with no mechanical cooling, using this strategy alone.

5.zoneStates: Perth metro, coastal NSW (Sydney to Newcastle), SE QLD coast (Brisbane)
5.summary: Mild and agreeable with warm to hot summers and cool winters. This is the most forgiving climate for passive design — mistakes are less costly. However, good passive design in this zone is still highly effective at eliminating the need for heating and cooling for the majority of the year.

principles.title: Face main living areas north
principles.body: Zone 5 has a clear seasonal sun angle difference — summer sun is high and northerly, winter sun is lower. North-facing living areas receive useful winter warmth and can be shaded in summer with modest eaves. This orientation is so effective in Zone 5 that a well-designed north-facing home with good glazing areas may need no supplementary heating at all, even in the coolest months. In Perth, aim for living areas within 30° of true north.

principles.title: 900–1000mm eaves on the north face
principles.body: In Perth (latitude 32°S) and Sydney (latitude 34°S), an eave depth of 900mm to 1000mm over standard 2.4m ceiling height windows provides near-complete summer shading while allowing useful winter sun penetration. This is one of the most precise and effective passive design solutions available. Shallower eaves (600mm or less) will allow summer overheating. External adjustable shade sails or blinds can supplement fixed eaves if your design has constraints.

principles.title: Moderate thermal mass in north-facing rooms
principles.body: A concrete slab, tiled floor, or masonry wall in north-facing living areas stores winter solar heat and releases it through the evening. In Zone 5's mild climate, even a modest amount of mass (exposed slab floor under tiles) makes a noticeable difference. Avoid heavy mass in rooms that do not receive winter sun — in those rooms it just stays cold. Polished concrete in a north-facing open-plan living area is an excellent choice.

principles.title: Protect the west wall in Perth especially
principles.body: Perth's hot afternoon sun from the west is the most significant source of summer discomfort. The afternoon sea breeze (the Fremantle Doctor) typically arrives between 2–4pm and provides relief, but until then the western sun is intense. Minimise glazing on the west face, or protect it with a pergola, deep eaves, external roller blinds, or planting. A deciduous grape vine on a western pergola is one of the most effective and aesthetically pleasing solutions in Perth.

principles.title: Capture the sea breeze or prevailing wind
principles.body: Zone 5 cities have reliable prevailing breezes that provide free cooling for much of summer if the house is designed to capture them. In Perth, the afternoon sea breeze arrives from the SW to SSW. Position openable windows or louvres on the windward side and outlet windows on the leeward side of each room. Rooms that are only one window-width deep ventilate much better than deep plan rooms. A courtyard design can funnel breezes into the centre of a deeper plan.

principles.title: Balance glazing area — not too much, not too little
principles.body: A common mistake in Zone 5 is over-glazing — large amounts of glass on the north, east, or west that create greenhouse conditions in summer. As a starting guide, north-facing glazing should be approximately 10–25% of the floor area of the rooms it serves, with appropriate eaves. South-facing glazing should be kept to a minimum (5% or less of floor area) as it provides no winter heat benefit but causes significant heat loss. Double-glazed windows pay off in this zone if your budget allows.

6.zoneStates: Melbourne, Hobart southern suburbs, Adelaide Hills, ACT lower elevations, parts of coastal Victoria
6.summary: Heating dominates the energy load in this zone. Winters are cool to cold, summers are warm but rarely extreme. Good passive solar design can eliminate most or all heating demand, which is the primary goal here. Summer cooling is secondary but still worth addressing.

principles.title: Passive solar heating is the primary goal
principles.body: In Zone 6, conventional house heating is a significant energy cost and environmental impact. A well-oriented passive solar home can reduce heating energy by 50–80% compared to a poorly oriented house. The investment in north orientation, correct eave sizing, and thermal mass pays back within a few years in energy savings. This is the single most important design decision in a Zone 6 home.

principles.title: Generous north-facing glazing — properly designed
principles.body: Zone 6 benefits from more north-facing glass than warmer zones — up to 25–35% of floor area for north-facing rooms. The additional glass area admits more winter sun to warm thermal mass. Critically, this glazing must be double-glazed (or better) — single glazing in Zone 6 loses more heat on cold nights than it gains on winter days, negating the benefit. Eaves must still be correctly sized to prevent summer overheating.

principles.title: Substantial thermal mass for winter heat storage
principles.body: Zone 6 requires generous thermal mass in north-facing rooms to store winter solar energy. A minimum of 80–100mm thick concrete slab exposed to direct winter sun is the starting point. Brick or stone feature walls behind north-facing glass add additional storage. The mass should be dark in colour to absorb more radiation. This stored heat releases through the evening, reducing the temperature drop overnight. A well-charged thermal mass can keep a Zone 6 home comfortable until 10–11pm without any supplementary heating.

principles.title: Double glazing is essential, not optional
principles.body: Single-glazed windows in Zone 6 are a significant liability. A single-glazed window loses roughly 10 times more heat per square metre than an insulated wall. In a Melbourne winter, this translates to substantial heating bills and persistent cold drafts near windows. Specifying double-glazed windows (minimum 6/12/6 or equivalent) is one of the highest-return passive upgrades in Zone 6. For south-facing windows, low-e coatings reduce radiant heat loss further.

principles.title: Seal draughts — they account for up to 25% of heat loss
principles.body: In Zone 6 homes, uncontrolled air infiltration (draughts through gaps around doors, windows, exhaust fans, cornices, and skirting boards) can account for a quarter of all winter heat loss. Draught sealing is one of the cheapest and most effective improvements for existing homes. For new builds, specify continuous air barriers in the wall construction and draught-sealed windows and doors as a baseline. Supply adequate controlled ventilation separately — you need fresh air but you want to control when and where it enters.

principles.title: Design for summer ventilation even though heating dominates
principles.body: Zone 6 summers are warm and occasionally have heatwave days in the high 30s to low 40s (particularly Melbourne). A well-insulated, draught-sealed house that handles winter well can overheat quickly without the ability to ventilate in summer. Ensure all north-facing windows are openable, include high-level windows or skylights for stack effect ventilation on still days, and install ceiling fans in living areas and bedrooms. External shading on north-facing glass is still essential to prevent summer overheating.

7.zoneStates: Tasmania (Hobart, Launceston), ACT highlands, alpine Victoria and NSW, Blue Mountains
7.summary: Cold winters with frequent frosts, and mild to cool summers. Heating is the overwhelming energy challenge — a Zone 7 home without good passive solar and insulation will be cold and expensive to heat year-round. Summer is not a significant cooling problem. Every design decision should prioritise winter warmth.

principles.title: North orientation is critical — no compromise
principles.body: In Zone 7, a south-facing or poorly oriented home can be almost impossible to heat passively and will cost significantly more to heat mechanically. North orientation for all habitable rooms is the single most important decision. In Hobart and Canberra at latitude 42°S and 35°S respectively, the winter sun is very low in the sky and stays firmly in the northern arc — a south-facing house may receive almost no direct sun for four months of the year.

principles.title: Superinsulate — go beyond minimum requirements
principles.body: Zone 7 demands the highest insulation levels. Ceiling insulation of R6.0–R7.0 is strongly recommended (minimum code may be lower but the energy savings from going higher are significant). Wall insulation of R3.0–R4.0. Slab edge insulation to prevent thermal bridging. Under-slab insulation if the slab is on ground. Triple-glazed windows perform well in the most exposed locations. The principle is simple: every bit of heat generated inside the house should stay there as long as possible.

principles.title: Specify double or triple glazing throughout
principles.body: Single glazing in Zone 7 is a serious thermal liability. Double glazing is the minimum appropriate specification. For south-facing windows and for the coldest highland locations, triple glazing is worth considering. Thermally broken aluminium frames or uPVC frames prevent the frame itself from conducting cold into the room — in cold climates, frame thermal performance matters nearly as much as the glass specification.

principles.title: Thermal mass only where winter sun directly reaches
principles.body: Thermal mass stores heat when it is in direct sunlight, but in the absence of sun it simply becomes a large cold object that takes energy to warm up. In Zone 7, place thermal mass only in rooms that receive direct winter sun for at least 4–5 hours per day. The living area with a north-facing glazed wall is the right place. Bedrooms and south-facing rooms should use lightweight construction so they warm up quickly from the heating system rather than slowly from a mass that is perpetually cold.

principles.title: Minimise south-facing windows to the absolute minimum
principles.body: South-facing glazing in Zone 7 provides almost no solar gain and is a significant source of heat loss. Keep south-facing windows to the legally required minimum for natural light and ventilation. Where south windows are unavoidable (view, site constraints), specify triple-glazed low-e glass and install good quality thermal curtains or blinds that seal to the wall at the sides to prevent convective heat loss from the glass to the room at night.

principles.title: Summer shading is largely unnecessary — focus entirely on winter
principles.body: Unlike warmer zones, Zone 7 summers are mild. Eaves on north-facing windows still provide some summer benefit but need not be as deep as in Zone 4 or 5 — the summer sun is high enough to be naturally excluded by shorter eaves. Spending your design budget on summer shading devices in Zone 7 is largely wasted effort. Prioritise insulation, glazing quality, draught sealing, and thermal mass exposed to winter sun.

8.zoneStates: Alpine areas of Victoria and NSW (Thredbo, Falls Creek, Mount Hotham), highest elevations of Tasmania
8.summary: Extreme cold winters with heavy snowfall at the highest altitudes, and short mild summers. Heating is the total priority — this is the most demanding climate for building performance in Australia. Passive solar alone is insufficient; it must be combined with exceptional insulation, airtightness, and thermal mass to maintain habitable temperatures.

principles.title: Airtightness is as important as insulation
principles.body: In Zone 8 alpine conditions, wind-driven infiltration of cold air is a major heat loss pathway. A leaky house at 1500m altitude with winter temperatures of -10°C can be almost impossible to heat economically. Aim for airtight construction: continuous air barriers on external walls, taped window-to-frame joints, sealed service penetrations, and pressure-tested draught-proofing throughout. Install a heat recovery ventilation (HRV) system to provide fresh air without energy loss — in this zone, HRV is not a luxury.

principles.title: Maximum insulation everywhere
principles.body: Zone 8 demands the highest insulation levels available. Roof/ceiling: R7.0 or higher. Walls: R4.0–R5.0. Under-slab: R2.5 minimum. Slab edge: fully insulated. Thermal bridging through structural elements (steel studs, slab edges, window frames) is a major issue — every bridge must be broken with appropriate thermal break materials. Structural insulated panels (SIPs) or double-stud timber framing can achieve the wall insulation levels required.

principles.title: Triple glazing is the appropriate standard
principles.body: Double glazing is the minimum in Zone 8; triple glazing is the appropriate standard for exposed locations. Thermally broken frames are not optional — standard aluminium frames conduct cold into the building and create condensation and icing on interior surfaces. uPVC or timber frames with triple glazed units perform significantly better. High-performance curtains or internal insulated shutters over all windows on cold nights provide significant additional benefit.

principles.title: Compact building form reduces heat loss
principles.body: Heat loss is proportional to external surface area. A compact building form (ideally approaching a cube) has the lowest surface-area-to-volume ratio and therefore loses the least heat relative to the space inside. Avoid long narrow floor plans, complex roof forms with multiple ridges and hips, and excessive roof overhangs that increase exposed roof area. Every square metre of external surface you can eliminate is energy you will not have to spend heating the inside.

principles.title: North-facing passive solar is helpful but not sufficient alone
principles.body: Passive solar design still works in Zone 8 — a north-facing house with good glazing and thermal mass will be warmer and cheaper to heat than a poorly oriented one. However, in alpine climates with heavily overcast winters and frequent snow (which can cover north-facing glazing), passive solar alone cannot maintain comfort. It should be combined with a highly efficient active heating system (hydronic radiant floor heating, efficient wood or pellet heater) as backup and supplement.

principles.title: Design for snow loads and associated risks
principles.body: Snow accumulation on roofs and around foundations creates unique design requirements in Zone 8. Steep roof pitches (minimum 25°, ideally 35°+) allow snow to shed naturally and prevent ice dam formation. Wide eaves must be carefully designed to handle snow loads without collapse. North-facing glazing should be steeply raked (60°+) to prevent snow accumulation on glass. Entry areas need protection from snow falling from the roof. These are structural considerations that must be integrated into the passive design from the start.
